prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

- **Strong narrative arc**: Clear progression from overconfidence → humbling reality → productive pivot to debate. The structure is easy to follow and satisfying.
- **Authentic voice**: Feels genuine throughout—honest about embarrassment without being overly dramatic or self-pitying.
- **Specific details**: "pick and roll," the scrimmage, the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team. These ground the story and make it memorable.
- **Meaningful reflection**: Goes beyond "failure is bad" to articulate real lessons—the difference between casual and competitive play, and how failure can redirect you toward better opportunities.
- **Good landing**: The final paragraph about "you don't know what you don't know" is genuinely insightful.

## What Could Improve

- **Word count is short**: ~400-430 words vs. the ~500 target. Could be tightened up slightly or expanded with more detail.
- **Show more, tell less**: Phrases like "I was really upset for a while" could be conveyed through specific actions or moments instead.
- **The tryout details are rushed**: The middle section moves quickly. More sensory details or specific moments during the three days would make it more vivid.
- **Underdeveloped contrast with Marcus**: Why did he succeed? A bit more exploration here would strengthen the narrative.
- **Could say more about debate**: A sentence or two showing why debate fit better would reinforce the conclusion.

## Bottom Line

This is a solid, honest essay that fulfills the assignment well. It has real insight, good structure, and an authentic voice. It just needs a bit more detail and length to be excellent.

This is solid personal narrative writing with genuine voice and clear structure. It just needs to be slightly longer and give a bit more weight to the resolution.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative arc** – Clear setup, rising action through tryouts, the pivotal scrimmage moment, and a satisfying reflection. This reads like a complete story.

**Authentic voice** – It sounds like a real person writing honestly. No pretension or forced language.

**Specific details** – "Pick and roll," the kid telling you to stay out of the way, Marcus making the team—these concrete moments make it feel real rather than generic.

**Genuine reflection** – The final paragraphs show real thinking, especially the insight about "you don't know what you don't know." It's not a canned lesson.

**The debate pivot** – The unexpected turn keeps this from being a predictable "I failed and tried again" story.

## What Could Be Stronger

**Show, don't tell** – "I was really upset for a while" tells us, but a concrete detail would hit harder. What did you do? How did it manifest?

**The debate section feels underdeveloped** – You mention it changed your high school experience but only give one line about why it clicked ("I was better at arguing than dribbling"). Why did debate work when basketball didn't?

**Missing sensory details in key moments** – The scrimmage is the emotional climax, but we don't *feel* what it was like standing there. What did the gym sound like? How did your body feel?

**The ending is slightly neat** – Real failures often have more complexity. You've resolved this very cleanly, which is fine, but acknowledging some ongoing feelings would add depth.

## Minor Notes
"Obviously" at the start of a paragraph feels a bit dismissive of what should be an emotional moment.

This is solid work—it hits the assignment well with good voice and genuine insight.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative foundation.** You have a clear story arc with specific, concrete details—the driveway games with Marcus, not knowing what a pick and roll was, the scrimmage where someone told you to stay out of the way. These details make the story real and memorable rather than generic.

**Authentic voice.** This sounds like a real person reflecting, not someone trying to sound fancy. Lines like "which sounds like a weird pivot" feel genuine and conversational.

**Genuine insight.** Your final lessons move beyond "failure is sad" to something more nuanced: the difference between casual and competitive play, how failure can redirect you toward something better, and the humbling realization that "you don't know what you don't know." These are actually thoughtful takeaways.

**Good length and scope.** You cover the full arc without being scattered—setup, failure, aftermath, growth.

## What Could Improve

**Reflection feels tacked on.** The last two paragraphs do a lot of heavy lifting with the lessons. Try weaving your reflections throughout the story rather than stacking them at the end—it would make the essay feel more integrated.

**The debate pivot deserves more.** You mention joining debate, but it's rushed. What was that first meeting like? Why did it click? This is where the failure actually *led* somewhere, so it deserves more weight.

**More sensory detail in the moment.** The scrimmage scene is good but could hit harder. What did you physically feel? The embarrassment, the adrenaline, the moment someone said "stay out of the way"? Let us be there.

**Minor language notes:** Some phrases slip into cliché ("it's just information," "sounds like a weird pivot"). Your strongest writing is when you're most specific.

---

**Overall:** This is solid, honest work that fully meets the assignment. Your friend should be proud of it.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Strong voice and structure.** This reads naturally—like a real person reflecting, not someone trying too hard. The narrative has a clean arc: setup → failure → realization → pivot → reflection.

**Specific details make it vivid.** "Pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way," the driveway games with Marcus—these concrete moments ground the story and make it believable.

**Multiple, genuine lessons.** Rather than one obvious takeaway, your friend identifies several layers: casual ≠ competitive, failure can redirect you somewhere better, and "you don't know what you don't know." That last one especially shows real insight.

**Good humor and perspective.** Lines like "I was better at arguing than dribbling" and the honest reflection about the kid who was mean but right—these add personality without being cheesy.

## What Could Be Stronger

**More sensory detail.** The scrimmage scene could use *showing* rather than telling. What did the gym sound like? How did it feel physically to be outmatched? This would make it more immersive.

**The Marcus comparison gets dropped.** It's interesting that his friend made the team, but it's not really developed or revisited—it could either be cut or explored more.

**Slightly summary-ish in places.** "I was really upset for a while" tells us, but showing a specific moment of disappointment would hit harder.

**The pivot to debate feels a bit fast.** One sentence explains joining debate; a bit more detail about *why* he chose that would help.

---

**Bottom line:** This is solid work. It's genuine, well-structured, and hits the assignment. Just needs a bit more texture and a couple moments of deeper scene-setting to move from good to great.
